title: Earnings Extrapolation and Predictable Stock Market ReturnsOpen Access
abstract: The U.S. stock market’s return during the first month of a quarter correlates strongly with returns in future months, but the correlation is negative if the future month is the first month of a quarter, and positive if it isn’t. These correlations offset, consistent with the well-known near-zero unconditional autocorrelation, yet they are pervasive, present across industries and countries. The pattern accords with a model in which investors extrapolate announced earnings to predict future earnings, not recognizing that earnings in the first month of a quarter are discretely less predictable than in prior months. Survey data support the model.

article.1. Other Related Literature:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Lauren Cohen Lauren Cohen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Predicting stock market returns with past returns is famously difficult.Kendall and Hill (1953)andFama (1965)documented decades ago that serial correlations in stock returns are close to zero. In his seminal work,Fama (1970)defines the weakest form of the efficient market hypothesis as that prices reflect all information in past prices. This hypothesis appears to work especially well for the U.S. aggregate market.Poterba and Summers (1988)show, for example, that monthly market returns in the United States have a small, insignificantly positive autocorrelation over a horizon of 12 months. The lack of correlation between past and future market returns in the United States is not only a widely accepted statistical phenomenon, but also an emblem of market efficiency.1

I document that the U.S. market return in the first month of a quarter in fact strongly correlates with returns in future months, with the correlation being strongly negative if the forecasted month is the first month of a quarter (i.e., January, April, July, and October) and strongly positive if it is not. This result can be understood as a two-step refinement of regressing 1 month’s return on the past 12 months’ return. The first step is to condition on the timing of the dependent variable. The market return in the first month of a quarter is strongly negatively predicted by the return in the preceding 12 months, whereas the returns in the second and third months of the quarter are strongly positively predicted by the past 12 months’ return. This distinction is already strong, highly significant at the 1% level, but it becomes even stronger after taking the second step—refining the independent variable. Specifically, among any 12 consecutive months, exactly 4 are the first months of a quarter, and these 4 months are especially useful for predicting future returns.

These first months of a quarter are special and important to investors because they contain fresh earnings news about the aggregate economy. This is owing to the nature of the earnings cycle in the United States. Take January as an example. At the end of the December, firms close their books for Q4, and they announce Q4 earnings in January, February, and March. January therefore contains the early earnings announcements and is the first time that investors learn about the economy’s performance in Q4. February and March also contain a sizable fraction of earnings announcements, but by that time investors have already learned much about Q4. The first months of the quarters are famously known as the “earnings seasons” among practitioners and receive heightened attention. Throughout this paper, I refer to them as “newsy” months, as they produce fresh earnings news. I call the other 8 months “non-newsy” months. This terminology allows me to compactly describe the above pattern of market return predictability in one sentence: newsy month returns negatively predict future newsy month returns, and positively predict future non-newsy month returns.

I hypothesize that this pattern arises from imperfectly rational investors who extrapolate newsy month earnings to predict earnings in future months, but fail to account for subtle variations in such predictability across those future months. Because earnings autocorrelate across multiple fiscal quarters, the practice of extrapolation is broadly correct. Furthermore, investors in my framework also correctly understand that the predictive power of earnings in a newsy month declines over the forecasting horizon. However, the speed of this decline is not a constant. In fact, all decline occurs when the forecasted months are newsy, creating a step function. This is because in newsy months, firms announce earnings of a new fiscal period, and these earnings naturally have a lower resemblance with past earnings. A rational investor should account for this time-varying speed of decay when forming expectations. However, if the investors incorrectly assume that this speed is a constant, then positive news in the past predicts negative surprises in future newsy months and positive surprises in future non-newsy months. These predictable surprises then give rise to predictable return reversal (continuation) in newsy (non-newsy) months. Furthermore, even as investors naively extrapolate, their expectations will tend to shift more in newsy months, making the returns in those months especially useful in predicting future returns.

Having stated my hypothesis, I test its predictions. I first show with survey data from IBES that sell-side analysts indeed fail to account fully for this variation in earnings autocorrelation, consequently leaving different types of predictability in earnings surprises throughout the earnings cycle. This evidence is uniquely useful because survey data are direct measures of market expectations, albeit imperfect. Moreover, I show that the predictable reversals in market returns do not occur until the earnings seasons begin. This alignment in timing suggests that it is indeed the earnings seasons that drive the return predictability, as opposed to other quarterly fluctuations unrelated to earnings announcements.

In addition to predictions confirmed in the time-series setting, my theory also makes predictions about cross sections. In particular, the imperfect extrapolation mechanism that I propose should additionally apply to the cross sections of industries and countries. Motivated by these natural extensions, I uncover a similar return predictability pattern in the cross section of industry excess returns.2Continuation in the cross section of stock returns, as inJegadeesh and Titman (1993), has also been extensively studied. Unlike the weak continuation found in the U.S. aggregate market, momentum in excess stock returns is a much stronger and more robust effect (e.g.,Asness, Moskowitz, and Pedersen 2013), and the industry component is shown to drive a large fraction of it (Moskowitz and Grinblatt 1999). This paper shows that the strength of the continuation in the cross section of industry returns also varies over time. Similar to the results found in the aggregate market, industry momentum is strong in the non-newsy months but virtually nonexistent in the newsy months. Moreover, this dynamic momentum pattern is very strong in industries with tightly connected fundamentals and weak in loosely connected industries, as my theory predicts. Additionally, I find a similar pattern in country momentum (Moskowitz, Ooi, and Pedersen 2012;Goulding, Harvey, and Mazzoleni 2023). This consistency across multiple contexts is remarkable and not always seen in studies of return predictability. These cross-sectional results not only expand the breadth of this paper but also provide important out-of-sample evidence.3

I also note that it seems difficult to explain the return predictability results with a risk-based framework. Presumably, in such a framework, after a good newsy month the future newsy months would be safe, but the future non-newsy months would be risky. Moreover, expected market returns implied by my results are frequently negative, so, consequently, a risk-based explanation would imply that the stock market is frequently safer than cash. However, common macro-based risk measures such as the surplus consumption ratio (Campbell and Cochrane 1999) do not vary strongly at the monthly frequency, and even if they did, they would unlikely be able to make the stock market safer than cash. Therefore, the most natural explanation for the novel predictability documented in this paper involves investors who make predictable mistakes. Such an explanation likely resides in the area of behavioral finance.

My empirical findings have direct and significant implications for an important question: how predictable is the aggregate stock market return? A vast body of literature dedicated to this very question currently views the stock market as either entirely unpredictable (Goyal and Welch 2008) or very weakly predictable with out-of-sampleR2s in the order of tens of basis points (Campbell and Thompson 2008).Goyal, Welch, and Zafirov (2024)surveyed recent studies in this literature and reaffirm this view. By documenting a statistically strong, easily tradable, and pervasively observed predictor of aggregate stock market that produces an out-of-sampleR2of approximately 4%, my paper significantly shifts the literature’s prior on the degree to which the aggregate stock market return is predictable. Additionally, my empirical results also show that three important and commonly made assumptions about stock market returns are not innocuous: i) the expected stock market return is persistent, ii) the expected stock market return almost always exceeds the risk-free rate, and iii) the stock market return is covariance-stationary.

From a theoretical angle, my mechanism of expectation formation belongs to the broader literature of fundamentals extrapolation (Lakonishok, Shleifer, and Vishny 1994;Greenwood and Hanson 2013;Bouchaud et al. 2019);Nagel and Xu (2022). An important mechanism behind extrapolative practices is diagnostic expectations (Bordalo et al. 2019;Bordalo et al. 2020;Bordalo et al. 2021). Investors who form expectations diagnostically overestimate the probabilities of the states that have recently become more likely, resulting in overextrapolation on average. In my framework, investors also have extrapolative beliefs, but the level of extrapolation is correct on average. The level is incorrect in a specific month, as the earnings announced in that month will have either higher or lower correlation with past earnings than expected. Under- and overextrapolation coexist in my framework. They just target earnings of different months.

Behind my extrapolative framework lies a key mechanism where investors’ limited cognitive capacity prevents them from fully exploring the richness of the parameter space driving the data generating process (i.e., the decay speed of the relevance of a newsy month’s earnings to future months’ earnings across these future months), leading them to simply use a moderate representative value instead. Analogous mechanisms of “parameter compression” are being actively studied by a burgeoning literature in different contexts.Matthies (2021)finds that beliefs about covariance exhibit compression towards moderate values in the context of natural gas and electric futures, as well as macroeconomic forecasts.Wang (2021)documents autocorrelation compression in the context of the yield curve.Fan, Liang, and Peng (2021)experimentally study simplifying heuristics that investors adopt when forming expectations.Augenblick, Lazarus, and Thaler (2025)use both experimental and empirical data to show that investors do not fully explore the heterogeneity in strength of signals. Such parameter compression mechanism can further be micro-founded in a limited attention mechanism surveyed inGabaix (2019). In Section 2.3.5,Gabaix (2019)points out that agents may incorrectly compress autocorrelations among a group of series towards their mean due to limited attention. My paper contributes to this nascent literature by being the first to apply its core mechanism of parameter compression to the stock market and documenting its important empirical implications.

This mechanism of parameter compression has another significant implication for the literature of behavioral finance.Fama (1998)presents an important critique of behavioral finance, noting that, in this literature, return reversal and continuation appear about equally frequently, and the evidence accords with the efficient market hypothesis if viewed together. This critique emphasizes the importance of articulating the circumstances in which agents would under- rather than overreact. The parameter compression mechanism provides a pathway to this objective by incorporating under- and overreaction in the same framework. When signals exhibit heterogeneous degrees of relevance to a forecasting target—as seen inAugenblick, Lazarus, and Thaler (2025)—compressing this relevance across signals could lead to underreaction to the high-relevance signals and overreaction to the low-relevance ones. My paper, along withMatthies (2021)andWang (2021), offers a distinct yet complementary perspective by unveiling a surprising possibility: even if investors only look at a single signal, they can simultaneously underreact and overreact to it. These seemingly contradictory reactions coexist because, in my framework, even though investors focus on one signal at a time, they use the signal to forecast multiple targets for which it has heterogeneous relevance. They underreact when forecasting one type of target and overreact when forecasting another type. Furthermore, investors in my framework do not compress the signal’s relevance into a single value, but rather into a simpler (but nonconstant) function across the targets. This generalization widens the scope of the parameter compression mechanism and takes a step forward to new findings on this topic.
This study broadly relates to a few areas of existing literature beyond those mentioned earlier. If viewed from a purely empirical angle, my paper makes the point that breaking the dependent variable into two pieces can potentially uncover rich variation in the behavior of an autoregression of returns. An analogous point is made byLou, Polk, and Skouras (2019), who break standard close-to-close daily stock returns into the close-to-open and open-to-close parts, and show that the former positively correlates with past stock returns, while the latter negatively correlates with past returns. My paper makes an additional point that breaking the independent variable into two pieces can highlight the difference in how they predict future returns. In a recent paper,Lou, Polk, and Skouras (2022)make an analogous point by showing that close-to-open and open-to-close stock market returns predict future returns very differently.

My paper also relates to work on predictable yet eventually surprising fundamental changes (e.g.,Hartzmark and Solomon 2013;Chang et al. 2017). This line of work shows that events that discretely yet predictably break from the past (e.g., issuing of large dividends or having seasonally high earnings) can still surprise investors and lead to positive excess returns on the corresponding firms. Extending this intuition to the aggregate market, one can understand the earnings announcements early in the earnings cycles as trend-breaking events, as the announced earnings have a discretely yet predictably lower correlation with past earnings. If investors also fail to anticipate this drop, we will observe reversals of market returns in the newsy months.

A feature of my model is that investors do not fully appreciate the correlation between earnings revealed early and late in the earnings cycle.Enke and Zimmermann (2019)show in an experimental setting that subjects fail to discount correlated news despite the simple setup and extensive instructions and control questions.Fedyk and Hodson (2023)document that old “news” on Bloomberg terminals—especially news items that combine multiple sources and are not direct reprints—generates overreactions in the abnormal returns of the associated stocks that are corrected in subsequent days. The continuation arm of my results shares this theme, even though my results are on the aggregate level and over a longer horizon (monthly).4It is worth noting that, even though the literature of correlation neglect and predictable fundamentals can individually explain the continuation and reversal arms of the returns, having a single explanation (i.e., parameter moderation) for both arms still adds meaningfully to the behavioral finance literature.

This paper also falls into the broader literature that studies the interaction between earnings announcements and stock returns (e.g.,Beaver 1968;Bernard and Thomas 1989;Bernard and Thomas 1990;Han 2019;Chen, Cohen, and Wang 2020;Johnson, Kim, and So 2020). An important piece of recent work in this area isSavor and Wilson (2016), which focuses on weekly stock returns. The authors first confirm that stocks have high returns on the earnings announcement week (Beaver 1968) and additionally show that stocks that have high announcement week returns in the past are likely to have high announcement week returns in the future. Overall, the authors argue that earnings announcements of individual firms resolve systematic risks and therefore earn risk premia.

Instead of focusing on the risks and the mean (excess) returns associated with earnings announcements, I focus on the misreaction associated with them and the resulting lead-lag relation of stock returns. Also, instead of focusing on the returns of the portfolio that longs the announcing firms and shorts the nonannouncing firms, I focus on aggregate market returns and industry returns in excess of the market, neither of which strongly correlates with the spread between the announcing and nonannouncing portfolios. In addition to these high-level distinctions, specific differences in empirical results will be further discussed later inSection 7.

This paper also relates to the broad literature studying the seasonality of stock returns, as documented byHeston and Sadka (2008),Keloharju, Linnainmaa, and Nyberg (2016), andDa and Zhang (2024). This literature also studies the autocorrelation of stock returns, and makes the point that full-year lags have especially strong predictive power, which is a distinction of the independent variable. The main point of my paper, however, is that the predictive power of past returns is different according to the timing of the dependent variable. Philosophically, seasonality is consistent with the notion of stationarity of stock returns, whereas my paper challenges it—specifically, the notion that the autocorrelation coefficients depend on displacement and not time. Again, specific distinctions will be further discussed inSection 7.

This paper belongs to the literature studying the information conveyed by early earnings announcements, where many works reside in the accounting area.Foster (1981)documents that earnings announcements have a strong impact on the stock prices of other firms in the same industry.Clinch and Sinclair (1987)confirm and extend this result to a sample of Australian firms;Han and Wild (1990)andHann, Kim, and Zheng (2019)confirm similar results using alternative measures of information andHan, Wild, and Ramesh (1989)andBrochet, Kolev, and Lerman (2018)do so with alternative announcements.Gilbert, Hrdlicka, and Kamara (2018)first document the importance of understanding asset pricing models along with the structure of earnings release.Chen, Cohen, and Wang (2020)show that, during the newsy months, days when famous firms announce their earnings have high average stock market returns. These works highlight the importance of early earnings announcements. Relatedly, the literature has also documented that investors’ learning from these early earnings announcements is often imperfect and leads to predictable errors:Ramnath (2002)documents that news announced by the first announcer in the industry positively predicts surprises in subsequent announcements in the same industry, and argues that investors underreact to these first announcements.Thomas and Zhang (2008)confirm this finding but additionally point out that, within the same industry, the late announcers’ excess returns during early announcers’ announcements negatively predict these late announcers’ own announcement excess returns. This suggests that investors overreact to these early announcements.5My paper differs from this literature in several aspects: It focuses on industry-level excess returns as opposed to stock-level in the context of both the independent and dependent variables. Also, the frequency I focus on is monthly as opposed to daily. Finally, I am interested in both intra-quarter and cross-quarter relations as opposed to just the former.
article.2. Data:
In terms of data sources, in the United States, my return data come from CRSP, accounting data come from Compustat North America, and EPS forecast data come from IBES Detail History (adjusted). These are common data sources used by a large number of studies. There are two main sources of earnings announcement dates in the United States: Compustat and IBES. Both data sources have been individually used in major studies. These announcement days often disagree, though the disagreements are usually small and are mostly concentrated before December 1994 (Dellavigna and Pollet 2009). For my purpose, this source of discrepancy is unlikely to make a difference, but out of an abundance of caution, I implement the algorithm inDellavigna and Pollet (2009), which combines the Compustat and IBES announcement dates to form the best estimate of the actual announcement dates.6In the event that the adjusted announcement date is the same as the IBES announcement date, I also shift the date to the next trading date if the IBES announcement time is after the market closure, followingJohnson and So (2018a).

Outside the United States, my stock-level return data mainly come from Compustat Global. The only exception is Canadian data, which come from Compustat North America. My country-level return data come from Global Financial Data (GFD). I use two different data sources of returns because GFD provides country-level return data with a longer history, and Compustat provides the stock-level data that I need for industry-level exercises. Compared to those focusing on the U.S. market, studies employing global accounting data and global earnings announcement dates are in smaller numbers. However, there are still many prominent papers (e.g.,Asness, Frazzini, and Pedersen 2019;Jensen, Kelly, and Pedersen 2023) from which two data sources emerge: Compustat Global and Thomson Reuters Worldscope. I compared the two data sources. Both of them have good coverage of annual accounting data, but Worldscope provides substantially better coverage of quarterly and semi-annual accounting data than Compustat Global. Also, Worldscope records about twice as many earnings announcements. These two factors are especially important for my purpose. I therefore use Worldscope for my study. I use exchange rate data from Bloomberg to convert foreign currency denominated accounting data and stock returns into U.S. dollar-denominated ones.

In terms of specific variables used, in the United States, I use “value-weighted average return” (vwretd) to represent aggregate market returns and SIC code to represent industries. I use “Report Date of Quarterly Earnings” (rdq from Compustat) and “Announcement Date, Actual” (anndats_act from IBES) for earnings announcement dates, “Common/Ordinary Equity–Total” (ceqq) for book value of equity, and “Income Before Extraordinary Items” (ibq) for earnings. Globally, I use item 5905 from Worldscope for earnings announcement dates, item 1551 for earnings, and item 7220 for book value of equity. Further variable construction details accompany specific analyses.7
article.3. Predicting the U.S. Aggregate Market:
In this section, I first describe the earnings cycle in the United States to show why the newsy months are newsy. I then demonstrate the return predictability pattern associated with these newsy months.

3.1. The earnings cycle and newsy months

The earnings cycle originates from i) aligned fiscal quarters and ii) heterogeneous announcement lags. Panel A ofTable 1shows that the vast majority of the U.S. firms—about 85% to be more precise—close their books for a given fiscal quarter on the last day of a calendar quarter. Consequently, from the perspective of the aggregate economy, the fiscal quarters over which the earnings are recorded are well aligned with the calendar quarters. While the fiscal quarters are aligned, firms do not announce their quarterly earnings together. Almost all firms announce within the 3 months after the fiscal quarter end date, but there is much variation in their announcement lags. Panel B ofTable 1shows that about 44% of the announcements occur in the first month of a quarter, about 43% in the second month, and about 13% in the last month.

The combination of the aligned fiscal quarters and the heterogeneous announcement lags gives the first month of a quarter a special status. It is the first time that investors learn about the aggregate economy’s performance in the previous quarter. This economy-level learning is possible because corporate earnings have a common time component: the aggregate economic condition in fiscal quarter 4 (FQ4) of 2021 influences all firms, and, consequently, if early announcers’ FQ4 earnings are good, then FQ4 is more likely than not a good quarter for everyone. The early announcers’ earnings therefore provide novel information for investors who wish to forecast FQ4 earnings of the late announcers, and—because earnings autocorrelate for multiple fiscal quarters—the economy’s earnings in future fiscal quarters such as FQ1 and FQ2 of the next year.

The first months of calendar quarters are newsy because they contain these early earnings announcements. The second months contain a similar number of earnings announcements, but by then investors have already learned much about the economy’s performance last quarter. Recognizing their importance, practitioners pay keen attention to earnings announcements during these newsy months, giving rise to the “street” notion of earnings seasons. It is worth noting that the newsy months are special for the purpose of inferring aggregate cash flows. If one is interested in a firm’s idiosyncratic cash flow, the corresponding earnings announcement is equally informative whether or not it occurs in the newsy months. Therefore, the first-order implications the newsy months have are for the aggregate market.

3.2. Baseline pattern of return predictability

Having established why these newsy months are important, I now describe the related predictability pattern in the U.S. aggregate market returns.Table 2reports results of the following monthly time-series regression that predicts the aggregate U.S. stock market return:mktt=α+∑j=14βjmktnm(t,j)+ϵt⁠. Here,mktnm(t,j)is thejth newsy month return strictly before the montht.Figure 1thoroughly illustrates how lagging is done on the regression. Suppose the dependent variable is the return of November, then lag 1 newsy month (abbrv. lag 1nm) return is that of October, lag 2nm return is that of July, and so on. As the dependent variable moves forward to December and January of the next year, the lagged newsy month returns on the right-hand side stay the same. However, when the dependent variable becomes the return of February, the lag 1nm return will move forward by 3 months to January, as that is the most recent newsy month strictly before February.

Having clarified the setup of the regression, I move on to the results. Column 1 ofTable 2does the regression above on the full CRSP sample and confirms the conventional view that the aggregate stock market exhibits only weak momentum with a look-back window of 1 year. Column 2 does the same regression, but only on the one-third of the sample where the dependent variable is returns of the newsy months. This column shows that, in newsy months, returns are decidedly negatively correlated with past newsy month returns. Column 3 does the regression for the rest of the sample, where the dependent variables are returns of non-newsy months. In those months, returns positively correlate with past newsy month returns. On average, the two arms offset, resulting in the weak unconditional predictive coefficients shown in column 1. The main empirical finding in this paper is that the serial predictive relation in stock returns varies by the timing of the dependent variable. Column 4 delivers this main point by showing the difference in the coefficients of columns 2 and 3. While the differences are not monotonic with lags, they are all negative, and overall the effect is stronger the smaller the lag.

To evaluate the strength of the effect in different contexts, such as different historical periods, it is useful to pivot on one coefficient instead of four. For the rest of the paper, I use the sum of the first four lags (i.e.,∑j=14mktnm(t,j)⁠) as my flagship signal. This lag number of four is not chosen to maximize performance. Rather, it corresponds to the typical 1-year look-back window of the various price momentum strategies in the literature. The alignment with past works imposes discipline and enables me to benchmark against those momentum strategies and speak to when they work and fail.

Here,∑j=14mktnm(t,j)is the sum of the past four newsy month returns, the said flagship signal. Its coefficient here roughly corresponds to the average of the first four coefficients inTable 2.8Column 1 shows the weak unconditional time-series momentum, pushed over thep-value cutoff of 5% by putting only the newsy month returns on the right-hand side (regressing on the past 12-month return will result in at-stat of 0.60). Columns 2 and 3 are the regressions with newsy months and non-newsy months as dependent variables, respectively. Again we see strong negative predictive coefficients in newsy months and strong positive coefficients in non-newsy months. The difference in the coefficients,−0.232⁠, is reflected by the coefficient of the interaction term in column 4 with at-stat of−4.93⁠. These two values reaffirm that, in the full CRSP sample of 1926–2021, the serial predictive relation of the U.S. aggregate market return is strongly varying by the timing of the dependent variable. 3.3. Implications for existing literature

After observing a pattern of return predictability, an important and logical first step is to evaluate its reliability—that is, one should first determine if the pattern is “real.” InSection A.1 of the Appendix, I show that the pattern is unlikely to be driven by data mining, outliers, or the Stambaugh bias, and that its performance has not diminished in recent history. Overall, the return pattern appears both historically reliable and currently relevant, warranting further investigation.

Having assessed the pattern’s reliability, I delve into its implications for the literature on return predictability. The return pattern documented in this paper shifts the literature’s prior on an important question: how predictable is the aggregate stock market return? SinceCampbell and Shiller (1988), this question has been at the heart of financial economics.Goyal and Welch (2008)surveyed and replicated decades of studies on this very topic. They conclude that existing signals “predicted poorly both in-sample (IS) and out-of-sample (OOS)…seem unstable…and would not have helped an investor with access only to available information to profitably time the market” (p. 1455). Specifically, they show that among 18 existing predictors of stock market returns, the monthly OOSR2range from−3.28to 0.22%, with a mean of−0.81⁠.9Campbell and Thompson (2008)argue that, after imposing regression and valuation constraints,10valuation ratios generate (mostly) positive monthly out-of-sampleR2ranging from−0.42to 0.97% with a mean of 0.32% across 22 forecasts.11Overall, the existing literature either views monthly stock market returns as entirely unpredictable or minimally predictable with an OOSR2in the range of tens of basis points even after imposing additional constraints. More than a decade after these two influential papers, this view appears firmly held, recently reaffirmed byGoyal, Welch, and Zafirov (2024).

InTable 4, I construct various forecasts of monthly market returns with real-time information and show that they generate out-of-sampleR2(à laGoyal and Welch 2008) from 3.6% to 4.3%. Further details regarding the calculation can be found inAppendix A.2. These numbers are approximately an order of magnitude higher than the OOSR2values documented inGoyal and Welch (2008)andCampbell and Thompson (2008), which are replicated and expanded inTable A2. In summary, the results inTable 4show that the stock market is in fact considerably more predictable than what the existing literature seems to believe.Appendix A.3further shows that the return predictability pattern documented in this paper can be effectively translated into profitable trading strategies in real time.

Additionally, my empirical findings also indicate that three important and commonly made assumptions concerning stock market returns are not innocuous: i) the expected stock market return is persistent, ii) the expected stock market return almost always exceeds the risk-free rate, and iii) stock market returns are covariance-stationary. Many of the predictors of stock market return documented in the existing literature lead to expected returns that are highly persistent and always exceed the risk-free rate. This feature perhaps reflects the prevalent assumptions that major economic risks are themselves persistent and that the stock market consistently carries more risk than Treasury bills (e.g., bySavor and Wilson (2016)andCampbell and Thompson (2008), respectively). The return pattern documented in my paper presents a surprising counter-example.Figure 2is a time-series plot of the out-of-sample expected stock market returns that stem from my predictor. Its monthly autocorrelation is slightly negative at−0.20⁠. Furthermore,Figure 2reveals that the expected stock market return is less than the Treasury bill rate 1 month out of 3. Finally, the return pattern in my paper shows that the monthly stock market returns are not covariance-stationary. Specifically, the auto-covariance of the monthly stock market returns depends not only on the distance between the two observations but also on whether they are newsy or not.

Overall, the return pattern documented in my paper significantly shifts the literature’s prior on a longstanding question in finance and challenges several key assumptions concerning stock market returns. In light of these important implications, it is worthwhile to seriously consider what causes this pattern.

3.4. Informative features of the return pattern

I note a few features of the data that are informative about what types of theories can potentially explain the pattern. An important and arguably most standard explanation for predictable returns is compensated risk. This is common to many leading asset pricing models (e.g.,Abel 1990;Constantinides 1990;Campbell and Cochrane 1999;Bansal and Yaron 2004;Bansal et al. 2014;Rietz 1988;Gabaix 2012;Wachter 2013). However, there are three reasons why my return pattern is unlikely to originate from compensated risks.

First, as discussed in the previous section, the pattern of return predictability leads to expected returns that vary at a high frequency.Figure 2is a time-series plot of the out-of-sample expected returns. Notice that these values oscillate on a month-to-month basis. This pattern is unsurprising: Assuming that returns of past newsy months are high, then a future month’s expected return is low if the month is newsy and high if it is non-newsy. In a risk-based framework, this implies that, after good newsy months, future newsy months are safe, but future non-newsy months are risky. Risk measures are unlikely to move in such a way, and even if they do, the high frequency of their movements is generally at odds with the leading asset pricing models cited above. These high-frequency movements can perhaps be accommodated in a risk-based framework, but it needs to depart substantially from past literature.

Also, as discussed in the previous section, the expected returns are less than the Treasury bill rates 1 month out of 3, and negative 1 month out of 5. In a risk-based framework, this means that the stock market is frequently safer than Treasury bills. This feature is especially difficult to accommodate in a risk-based framework, as it is implausible that investors find the stock market to be safer than Treasury bills that often (e.g.,Gómez-Cram 2022).

Finally, it seems that the reversal in the newsy months and the continuation in the non-newsy months go hand in hand in data.Figure 3separates the full CRSP history into decades and plots the strength of the continuation in non-newsy months against the strength of the reversal in newsy months. Specifically, it plotsβ1inTable A1againstβ1+β2⁠. Overall, there is a clear negative relation: in a decade where the continuation arm is strong, so is the reversal arm. This feature of the data tells us that it is desirable to have one story for both arms of the return pattern.12

Overall, these features suggest that it is unlikely that a risk-based framework alone can explain the return pattern because the expected returns i) have high-frequency movements of a bizarre nature and ii) take negative values too often. Furthermore, there should ideally be one story for both arms of the returns, as the two arms are clearly coupled in data.
article.4. Explaining the Return Pattern via an:
The preceding section shows that aggregate stock market returns in newsy months negatively predict future newsy month returns, and positively predict future non-newsy month returns. This difference in correlation persists across multiple newsy month lags. In this section, I employ an illustrative example to provide intuition for the reasons underlying this pattern of return predictability.

Consider investors who forecast aggregate earnings outcomes in the forthcoming months based on announced earnings. Let us assume that investors base their earnings forecasts only on earnings announced in the newsy months. This assumption reflects the earnings seasons and the heightened attention they receive (e.g.,Chen, Cohen, and Wang 2020).13To fix idea, suppose that investors have just observed positive earnings news in April from the announcing firms. Given this new signal, how should investors assess their forecasts for earnings in May, June, July, and subsequent months? Because earnings positively autocorrelate, earnings in the upcoming months are likely good. In fact, earnings announced in May and June are especially likely to be good. This is because the announcing firms in May and June mostly announce Q1 earnings, which is of the same fiscal quarter as the earnings announced in April. This zero fiscal quarter distance renders May and June earnings especially akin to those in April, so if April is good, May and June are especially likely follow suit.

However, in July, earnings of Q2, a different fiscal quarter, will be announced. Since earnings positively autocorrelate across multiple fiscal quarters, the number in July is still likely good, as Q2 and Q1 are only one fiscal quarter apart. However, earnings announced in May and June are zero fiscal quarter apart from those in April. Therefore, compared to those in May and June, earnings in July are less likely to resemble the good earnings in April. Similar drops in this resemblance also occur in October, the next January, and the next April.

Overall, the usefulness of the news in April in predicting earnings in the future months follows a step function across these future months, with the downsteps occurring in the newsy months. This is represented by the blue circles inFigure 4. In this figure, the “E” periods contain the earlier half of the earnings announcements for a quarter and are newsy. The “L” periods contain the latter half of the earnings announcements and are non-newsy. Investors stand at the end of period 0E, observe the earnings news from the announcements in 0E, and form expectations of the earnings that will be announced in period 0L, 1E, 1L, 2E, etc. As said, the predictive power of the 0E earnings on the earnings announced in these future periods is represented by the blue circles, which follow a step function with downsteps occurring in the E periods. InFigure 4, these blue circles decrease from the left to right across the forecasted periods, but the rate of this decay is not constant. In fact, all decay occurs in the E periods. This point is demonstrated with real earnings data inAppendix B.

The “E” periods represent the early half of the earnings cycle and are newsy. The “L” periods represent the late half of the earnings cycle and are non-newsy. The red circles represent the predictive power in investors’ belief. The green bars show their differences, representing investors’ forecasting errors. Open in new tab Download slide

Under the parameter compression hypothesis, investors instead adopt a simpler, smoother function in their expectation formation. In my example, the expectations are represented by the red crosses positioned along the fitted line across the blue circles. They decline with a constant speed over the forecasted periods, neglecting the time-varying parameter that is the alternating declining speed cross the E and L periods. This mechanism can be understood through the framework in Section 2.1–2.3 ofGabaix (2019), wherein investors’ optimization leads them to use the “default” speedxdthat is the average of the speed across the E and L periods.

The red crosses and blue circles have a similar mean over the forecasted months, and investors broadly understand that the predictive power of the 0E signal decays over time. However, neglecting the subtlety in the speed of this decline leads to predictable errors, represented by the green bars. They then give rise to predictable returns across multiple periods. Specifically, good returns in 0E would on average predict positive returns in 0L, negative returns in 1E, positive returns in 1L, negative returns in 2E, and so forth. Taken together, investors’ behavior proposed above can be compactly described as fundamentals extrapolation with a compressed parameter—investors incorrectly compress the time-varying decline speed among the blue circles inFigure 4to a constant. In the language of under- and overreaction, this imperfect extrapolation implies that investors overreact when forecasting the earnings announced in future newsy months, and underreact when forecasting earnings announced in future non-newsy months. As the forecasted earnings are later announced, the initial misreactions get corrected, and return continuation and reversal emerge. Thus, a single mechanism of parameter compression gives rise to both under- and overreaction, which in turn lead to both arms of the documented return predictability pattern.14

article.6. Testing the Hypothesis of Earnings Ex:
Having stated my hypothesis of earnings extrapolation with a compressed parameter, I further test it in this section. Four pieces of evidence are noteworthy: i) the alignment of the inception of predictable returns and early earnings announcements, ii) survey data, iii) the cross sections of industries, and iv) international stock markets. Below, I go through them individually.

6.1. First week of the quarter and first quarter of the year

First, in my previous analysis I focused on monthly stock returns and therefore used the concept of newsy months. However, the period with meaningfully many earnings announcements does not start immediately on the first day of the newsy months. This is because even the fast announcers need some time to prepare their statements. In fact, among firms with fiscal periods aligned with calendar quarters, only 0.27% of their earnings announcements occur within the first week (more precisely, the first 5 trading days) of the quarter. This is an order of magnitude lower than what an average week contains, which is about 8% of the announcements. In contrast, the second week in each quarter sees 2.93% of the announcements, which is on the same order of magnitude as an average week. The reversal of market returns—if indeed arising from these earnings announcements—should not exist in the first week of the newsy months.

Table 5shows that this prediction is true. While we have seen that past newsy month returns negatively predict the next newsy month return (reproduced in column 1), the coefficient is close to zero in the first week of a newsy month. This weak coefficient is shown in column 2. Consequently, excluding the first week of the newsy months makes the reversal effect even stronger, as shown in column 3. Overall,Table 5shows that the dynamic serial predictive relation in stock market returns is indeed tied to the timing of the early earnings announcements. This piece of evidence speaks in favor of stories involving these earnings cycles and against those built upon other unrelated quarterly fluctuations.

In addition to the first week of each quarter, in the first quarter of each year, we may also expect a weaker pattern in the dynamic serial correlation of returns. The reasons are twofold. First, in Q1 earnings announcing is substantially slower. In the United States, the median announcement lag in the first quarter is 41 days, whereas that for the other three quarters is 30 days. This is because firms need time to additionally conduct 10-K filing in Q1, which takes resources that could otherwise be used for earnings announcements. Hence, January is less newsy than the other 3 newsy months. Second, in Q1 widespread tax-related trading takes place, which can potentially add unrelated movements to aggregate returns. Both reasons can lead to a weaker effect in Q1 relative to the other three quarters.Table 6shows that this is true. Comparing columns 1–3 to 4–6, we see that in Q1 both the positive and negative arms of return predictability are weaker.

6.2. Evidence from survey data

6.2.1. Aggregate level evidence

In this subsection, I test my theory with survey data from IBES. In the model, investors overreact when predicting earnings in the upcoming newsy months, and underreact when predicting those in the upcoming non-newsy months. It therefore has a key prediction, which is that past earnings surprises positively predict surprises in the upcoming non-newsy months, and negatively predict those in the upcoming newsy months.

To test this prediction, I use EPS estimates from IBES Detail History. IBES Summary History conveniently provides firm-level consensus estimates, which are IBES’s flagship product. However, it cannot be used in this particular case because these consensuses are struck in the middle of the month per Thomson Reuters’s production cycle.18Since I use calendar month returns, I compute analogous consensus estimates at month ends, so that earnings surprises in a given calendar month can be measured relative to them.19

At the end of each month, each firm’s consensus EPS estimate for a given fiscal period is computed as the median of the most recent estimates issued by each analyst. Also, the calculation only includes estimates issued within 180 days. This horizon is chosen to reflect the methodology with which the IBES consensus is computed.20The results of the analysis are qualitatively the same with a wide range of look-back windows.

A firm’s earnings surprise in the announcing month is the announced EPS subtracting its consensus forecast at the end of the previous month, and then dividing by its stock price at the previous month end. I adopt price as the scaling variable to align with the standard practice in the literature that studies earnings surprises (e.g.,Hartzmark and Shue 2018). The results are similar if I instead use the book value of equity per share. For earnings announcements that occur in montht, I aggregate their surprise measures with a market capitalization weight to form the aggregate earnings surprise in montht, orSupt⁠. I then run time-series predictive autoregressions onSupt⁠, and, importantly, I separate the sample by whether the dependent variable is a newsy month and examine whether the predictive coefficients differ in the two subsamples.

Table 7shows that we indeed observe a significantly lower predictive coefficient when the dependent variable is newsy. Column 1 shows that past surprises strongly positively predict the eventual aggregate earnings surprise on average. Column 2 shows that the loading is less positive in the newsy months. Column 3 shows that it is much stronger in the non-newsy months. Column 4 shows that this difference between newsy and non-newsy months is statistically significant. Column 5 shows that this difference is especially strong if we use surprises in newsy months in the past to predict future aggregate earnings surprises. These significant differences are consistent with the key prediction of the model.

However, if the model is taken to be a literal model of survey data, we should not just expect a substantially weaker coefficient in column 2—we should see a negative coefficient, like in the regressions with aggregate market returns. We do not see this negative coefficient because the overall underreaction among the analysts, reflected by a strongly positive coefficient in column 1, is an aspect that does not translate into market returns—the aggregate market returns in the United States are not strongly positively autocorrelated overall. This discrepancy suggests that the latency in the survey expectations is not completely present in the actual expectations of the market,21and it is the latter that I model. A more realistic representation of survey expectation is perhaps an average between the timely market expectations and a smoothed measure of past expectations. In the wake of this modification, my model would generate this overall underreaction in the survey data. It would still predict that this underreaction is weaker in the newsy month, andTable 7shows that this key prediction holds true.

6.3. Cross-sectional analysis

The main intuition behind the theory is as follows. If a group of stocks are 1) tightly and obviously connected in fundamentals, so that investors actively infer group-level information from its members’ announcements and 2) sizable in number, so that the group as a whole announces progressively along the earnings cycle, then earnings announced in the newsy months will correlate discretely less strongly with past earnings, compared to earnings announced in the previous non-newsy months. Failure to recognize this stepped serial correlation structure of earnings leads to predictable surprises and predictable returns.

While it is natural to apply this story first to all stocks in the U.S. economy, it should additionally apply to the cross sections of industry-level stock returns. Two stocks randomly drawn from an industry are more connected with each other than two random stocks drawn from an economy. An industry is a smaller economy except more tightly connected. As discussed at the end ofSection 1, investors clearly understand the tight intra-industry connection and actively learn industry-level information from the early earnings announcements (e.g.,Foster 1981). If the story is indeed about the early earnings announcements, the dynamic serial predictive relation in the aggregate market returns should also exist in industry returns in excess of the aggregate market returns.

I construct industry-level returns from stock data in CRSP. In each cross section, I drop the small stocks, defined as those with market capitalization below the 10th percentile of the NYSE universe. I use the stock’s issuing company’s four-digit SIC code as the industry classification variable and drop observations with a missing SIC code or a code of 0, 9910, 9990, and 9999, which represent unclassified observations. Industry-level returns are market capitalization weighted. Twenty stocks are required in the industry-month. This size filter is applied because the theory requires a sizable group of stocks to be in an industry, and the results are qualitatively similar with alternative cutoffs.

A potential confounding effect of heterogeneity analyses of return predictability in general is that return predictability is naturally more concentrated on “inefficient” stocks and industries, such as those with low market capitalization, price, and volume because it is harder for arbitrageurs to take advantage of return predictability on these stocks. While there is no consensus on how this notion of inefficiency should be measured, market capitalization is a good place to start. It is worth noting that, among the sorting variables I use, within-industry cash flow connectivity and balance are not strongly related to industry capitalization. Entry cost and number of industry constituents are in fact positively related to industry capitalization, and the inefficiency story alone predicts smaller effects on the harder-to-enter and larger industries, which is the opposite of what my theory predicts and the opposite of what the data show. The tests inTable 10are sharp tests, in that the predicted empirical pattern is unlikely to arise from this other effect of heterogeneous “inefficiency.”

Finally,Appendix Cshows that similar return patterns can be found in a panel of international stock market returns, and the pattern broadly matches their (slower) earnings cycle. This piece of global evidence broadens my return predictability pattern and provides out-of-sample support to my theory.
article.7. Alternative Explanations:
In this section, I further discuss alternative explanations of the empirical patterns.

Firms endogenously changing their announcing latency.A large accounting literature examines the relation between the timing of firms’ earnings announcements and the news they convey. A clear empirical pattern that emerges is that early announcers tend to announce better news than late announcers (e.g.,Kross 1981;Kross and Schroeder 1984;Chambers and Penman 1984;Johnson and So 2018b;Noh, So, and Verdi 2021). The literature has also extensively studied the reasons behind this pattern and has partially attributed it to firms’ endogenous choices of the announcing lag. For instance,deHaan, Shevlin, and Thornock (2015)argue that firms delay earnings announcements with bad results to avoid the early portion of the earnings cycle that receives heightened attention.Givoly and Palmon (1982)argue that they do so to buy time so that they can manipulate their accounting results.

However, it is not clear how this empirical regularity alone speaks to my results. Overall, it implies that early announcers announce better results than late announcers. If investors fail to anticipate that, then early announcers will have higher earnings surprises and higher announcement excess returns. It is not clear why it would cause the market return early in the earnings cycle to positively correlate with that late in the earnings cycle. If anything, exogenous variation in the intensity of this self-selection effect seems to lead to a negative correlation of early announcement returns and late announcement returns within the quarter, as bad announcers being moved out of the newsy months makes the newsy months look better, and the subsequent non-newsy months look worse than they otherwise would. And even then, it is not clear why this return predictability pattern would extend across quarters.

Predictability reflecting predictable resolution of risks.The most important set of explanations for return predictability comes from predictable resolution of risks, which leads to high expected returns, as well as going through predictably low-risk periods, which leads to low expected returns. As discussed in the introduction andSection 3.4, the type of variation in risks that is necessary to explain my time-series results is that, after a good newsy month, the stock market is risky in the upcoming non-newsy months but safe in the upcoming newsy months. This first requires the risk of the market to vary at a monthly frequency. The variation needs to have a dependency up to four quarters away, and it needs to have a mixture of positive and negative dependence on the past, with the sign of the dependence going from positive to negative and then back to positive and so on. Second, the scale of such variation needs to be large, so that the expected stock market return is less than the Treasury bill rate 1 month out of 3. Such a scale is especially difficult to accommodate in a risk-based framework, as it is implausible for investors to view the stock market as an asset safer than Treasury bills, let alone 33% of the time. Therefore, I temporarily ignore the second point and discuss whether notable risk-based models can qualitatively relate to my return pattern.

Standard asset pricing models such as habit, long-run risk, disaster, and intermediary-based asset pricing all involve risks that should be rather persistent at a monthly frequency. Even if they do vary, it is unlikely that the variation would cause its dependency on the past to take alternate signs across months. So it does not appear hopeful to use them to explain my predictability pattern.

Another candidate is the risk resolution associated with earnings announcements. Empirically,Savor and Wilson (2016)show that early announcers earn higher excess returns than later announcers (Table IV), which is indeed consistent with the overall importance of the earnings season. This, however, does not explain why the current earnings season being good makes future earnings seasons safer and future months out of earnings seasons riskier. The authors also show that stocks with high past announcement returns in excess of the market earn higher returns during their upcoming announcement weeks than those with low past announcement excess returns (Table III). The authors argue that this shows that the level of risks resolved by a given stock’s earnings announcement is persistent. The authors also show that earnings announcement dates are highly stable, and therefore firms that have announced in January are likely to announce in April. When we combine these two pieces of information, it may seem that returns 3/6/9/12 months ago might be influencing part of my cross-sectional results. However, apart from the difference in return frequency (monthly versus weekly) and cross-sectional unit (industry/aggregate versus stock), observe that the excess returns 3/6/9/12 months ago are used only when the dependent variables are the newsy months, which is a subsample in which the coefficient in my regression is low, not high.

Savor and Wilson (2013)document that the equity premium on macroeconomic announcement days is especially large and argue that this is because it resolves important macroeconomic risks. Could this contribute to my pattern of return predictability in some way? If so, the return predictability would be realized on macroeconomic announcement days. A straightforward empirical investigation reveals that the predictability in fact works about 75% less well on the top five macroeconomic announcement dates on Bloomberg (i.e., FOMC, employment condition, GDP, PPI, and Institute for Supply Management manufacturing). The data suggest that the return predictability documented in this paper does not obviously relate to macroeconomic announcements.

Loading on the seasonality effect.My cross-sectional results might seem related to those inHeston and Sadka (2008)andKeloharju, Linnainmaa, and Nyberg (2016), who show that the full-year lags (i.e., the 12, 24, 36, … monthly lags) have unusually high predictive coefficients on stock-level excess returns. Since their signals have no time-series variation (i.e., stock returns in excess of the market have a zero mean cross section by cross section), the results cannot relate to my time-series pattern. However, could they explain my industry-level pattern? Full-year lags are indeed used in some of my regression results. Specifically, they are part of the right-hand side when the dependent variable is the first months of the quarter, as in column 2 ofTable 9. However, observe that the point of my paper is that when the predicted months are the first months of the quarter, the predictive coefficients are unusually low—exactly the opposite of the points made in the return seasonality literature. In other words, my results exist in spite of the loading on return seasonality, not because of it.
article.8. Conclusion:
Contrary to prior beliefs, monthly stock market returns in the United States can in fact be predicted with past returns. Specifically, the U.S. stock market’s return during the first month of a quarter correlates strongly with returns in future months, but the correlation is negative if the future month is the first month of a quarter, and positive if it is not. These first months of a quarter are “newsy” because they contain fresh earnings news about the aggregate economy. I hypothesize that the return predictability pattern arises because investors extrapolate these newsy month earnings to predict future earnings, but did not recognize that earnings in future newsy months are inherently less predictable than in previous months. Survey data support this hypothesis, as does out-of-sample evidence across industries and international markets—notably, the failure of industry momentum and country momentum in the newsy months. These results challenge the efficient market hypothesis by documenting a strong and pervasive form of return predictability.

Behind this pattern of predictability is a novel mechanism of expectation formation, which features fundamentals extrapolation with an overly simple, moderate parameter. This extrapolative mechanism with “parameter compression” gives rise to simultaneous under- and overreaction, a seemingly conflicting combination made possible by multiple forecasting targets: investors overreact when forecasting future newsy month earnings and underreact when forecasting future non-newsy month earnings. This mechanism conveys a more general message about the origin of under- and overreaction: investors underreact when forecasting something because they overreact when forecasting something else.

Appendix

A. Is the Return Predictability Pattern Reliable?

A.1. Robustness of the Return Pattern

Data mining is among the most common critiques made of the literature of return predictability (e.g.,Lo and MacKinlay 1990). I first note that the pattern seems unlikely to arise from pure chance or pure data mining, in part because thep-value associated with the key coefficient of –0.232 in column 4 ofTable 3is8.2×10−7⁠. Even if it was a cherry-picked result from multiple tests, for such ap-value to be considered insignificant with, say, the Bonferroni correction, one needs to have attempted 60,000 mutually independent tests, which, although possible, are implausibly many.

I then investigate whether the pattern is driven by outliers. On a decade-by-decade basis,Table A1performs the same regression as in columns 4–7 ofTable 3,mktt=α+β1∑j=14mktnm(t,j)+β2∑j=14mktnm(t,j)×Itnm+β3Itnm+ϵt⁠, whereItnmis a dummy variable taking the value of 1 if the dependent variable monthtis newsy.Table A1shows that in each and every decade of the CRSP history, there is a positiveβ1and, importantly, a negativeβ2⁠. Hence, the pattern seems generally present over time and not solely driven by a few observations. Unsurprisingly, the pattern is stronger in some decades than others. Specifically, it seems strong at the beginning and end of the sample. While such variation can easily be driven by noise, this empirical regularity does not seem to be going away in recent years, avoiding a criticismGoyal and Welch (2008)make of existing stock market predictors.

Next, I investigate whether this pattern is a result of a biased estimator. In his influential work,Stambaugh (1999)shows that in a predictive regression, when the error term of the dependent variable and contemporaneous innovation of the independent variable are correlated, a small-sample bias arises in the estimated coefficient. The bias increases with the persistence of the independent variable and casts particular doubt on the validity of aggregate valuation ratios as predictors of aggregate stock market returns. Because of its implication for market efficiency, the paper ignited a vigorous debate in the literature.24Following the calculation inStambaugh (1999), it seems the bias on the coefficient of –0.232 in column 4 ofTable 3is at most –0.006. Overall, it seems unlikely that the bias drives the return pattern.

A.2. Out-of-Sample Forecasts: Removing Look-Ahead Bias in Estimated Coefficients

Past research has cast doubt especially on the practicality of time-series strategies in the stock market. Specifically,Goyal and Welch (2008)show that in forecasting future stock market returns, predictors combined with expanding window coefficients extracted without look-ahead bias fail to outperform the expanding window mean of past market returns. WhileCampbell and Thompson (2008)show that imposing some simple and reasonable constraints on the regression estimation process will make the predictor-based approach clearly superior, it is nonetheless useful to make sure that the predictor in this paper can generate positiveR2without involving coefficients estimated with look-ahead bias.

To investigate theR2generated with no look-ahead bias coefficients, I extend the CRSP aggregate market return series to 1871 using GFD data. I take valuation ratios data from Robert Shiller’s website and construct payout ratios and ROE series with data from Amit Goyal’s website. These are used to generate the long-run return forecasts, as inCampbell and Thompson (2008). While the estimation sample goes back to 1872, theR2are evaluated starting from 1926, consistent with what is done inCampbell and Thompson (2008).

The monthlyR2s generated by various estimation methods are reported inTable 4. Coefficients are either constrained to be 1 or generated with simple expanding window OLS estimations. No shrinkage procedure is applied. In method 1, we see that even the most naive method—combining signal with regression coefficients of returns on past signals and a freely estimated constant—generates anR2of 3.65%. This number appears an order of magnitude higher than what are generated by other time-series signals for monthly U.S. stock market returns surveyed in the comprehensive study ofGoyal and Welch (2008)(and other studies, e.g.,Rapach and Zhou (2022)), the results of which are replicated/extended inTable A2. This highR2⁠, along with those generated with methods 2–7, mitigates the concern that the strategy implied by the time-series results in this paper could not have been profitably employed.

It is important to realize that this section is about the implementation of the coefficient estimation procedure. It makes no statement as to how much of these return predictability results will continue to exist in the future. Recent works explicitly making those statements includeMclean and Pontiff (2016),Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2020), andJensen, Kelly, and Pedersen (2023).

Columns with the “CT” prefix i) truncate the estimated coefficient on the signal at 0 if it has a different sign from that estimated on the full sample and ii) truncate the forecast at the risk-free rate if it exceeds the risk-free rate, as in Campbell and Thompson (2008) . A.3. Time-Series Strategy

I convert the return predictability patterns that I document into trading strategies implementable in real time. Coefficients obtained by regressing future returns on signals, such as those documented inTables 3and9, are in principle returns to long-short portfolios themselves (Fama and MacBeth 1973).25Hence, the results in this section are to some extent already implied by my return-predicting results. However, the implied portfolios often have look-ahead bias in their weights and are technically not implementable in real time. Out of an abundance of caution, in this section I form portfolios with information available in real time and compute the Sharpe ratio and multi-factor alphas.

Starting with the aggregate market strategy, at the end of each montht−1⁠, I take the total return of the aggregate market in the past four newsy months (which potentially include montht−1⁠) and compute the expanding window mean of this total return, which is its mean from the beginning of the sample to montht−1⁠. I then take the difference between the total return and its expanding window mean and flip the sign if monthtis newsy to arrive at the demeaned signalxt−1⁠. I then run a constrained time-series regressionmktt=βxt−1+1mkt―t−1+ϵt⁠, wheremkttis the market return in montht,xt−1is the said demeaned signal, andmkt―t−1is the expanding window mean of the market return up to montht−1⁠. The coefficient beforemkt―t−1is constrained to be 1. Denote the estimated coefficientβasct⁠. The forecasted market return at the end of monthtfor montht+1is thenctxt+mkt―t⁠. This is the forecast from method 6 ofTable 4. The portfolio weight in my strategy isctxt⁠, which roughly has a mean of zero over time. Finally, the portfolio is scaled by a constant so that it has the same volatility as the aggregate market, which is 5.34% per month.26This is set so that the portfolio’s average return is in units comparable to the aggregate stock market.

It is worth noting that the forecasting system that I use can be applied to a generic signal. The system also extends to a multivariate setting without having to worry about the difference in sample span for different signals; for missing signals one can just fill in a neutral value of 0. Here, I delegate the constant term in the regression to the expanding window mean of past returns. This is done to keep the forecasting system simple so that I observe my signal’s effect in isolation.Campbell and Thompson (2008)put forth better options for the purpose of optimal forecasting.

Table A3regresses the reported time-series portfolio returns on a constant and contemporaneous factors, including the market, value, size, and momentum. The coefficients on the constant are of interest. In column 1, the coefficient of 0.823 means that the portfolio has an average return of 0.823% per month, which is about 10% per annum. This corresponds to an annualized Sharpe ratio of 0.44, which is about the same as that on the aggregate market. It is worth noticing that explaining the sheer scale of this Sharpe ratio is non-trivial when the underlying instrument is just the aggregate market: the equity premium puzzle (Mehra and Prescott 1985) inspired sophisticated models developed by generations of scholars (e.g.,Abel 1990;Constantinides 1990;Campbell and Cochrane 1999;Bansal and Yaron 2004;Rietz 1988;Barro 2006;Gabaix 2012;Wachter 2013). Therefore the predictability I document is worth thinking about carefully.

In column 2, we see that the strategy is indeed roughly market neutral, as designed. It leads to a CAPM alpha of about 0.75% per month. Columns 3 and 4 show that the three- and four-factor alphas of this strategy are 0.64% and 0.74% per month. It is worth noting that even though my trading strategy involves substantial rebalancing, the underlying instrument is the aggregate market, and the incurred trading cost is likely lower than strategies that require stock-level rebalancing, such as the value, size, and the momentum factors.

A.4. Cross-sectional Strategy

Having discussed the time-series strategy, I now move on to the cross-sectional strategy trading on U.S. industries. For each industryiand montht, I computei’s total excess return of the past 4 newsy months and then cross-sectionally demean with the market capitalization weight at the end of montht. I then flip the sign of this total excess return if montht+1is newsy to arrive at the signalxi,t⁠. I then compute an expanding window standard deviationσtof the signalxacross all industry-months up to montht, weighting by the market capitalization divided by the total market capitalization of the cross section. The portfolio weight isxi,t/σtwinsorized at[−2,2]to make sure no extreme position is taken. Finally, the portfolio is then scaled so that it has the same volatility as the aggregate market excess return, which is 5.34% per month. Notice that this strategy is market neutral in each cross section.

Table A4regresses these cross-sectional portfolio returns on the market, value, size, and momentum factors. Column 1 shows that the average return of this portfolio is about 0.62% per month, and the annualized Sharpe ratio is about 0.33. Across the rows, we see that the multi-factor alphas of this strategy are strongly positive, even though they decline as factors are added because they have small positive loadings on the size and the momentum factors. It is worth noting that the factor’s loading on the momentum factor is not very strong. This is because it bets against continuation one-third of the time, operates at the industry level, and uses only newsy month returns as the predictors. Overall, this shows that the cross-sectional return predictability I document can also be translated into a trading strategy.

B. Earnings Dynamics in the United States

To illustrate the time-series dynamics of earnings, I first need a measure of the previously mentioned “earnings” for a given month and ideally without using return information. It would be especially helpful to explain the pattern in stock returns without using stock returns. The measure I choose is aggregate return on equity (ROE), which is aggregate quarterly earnings divided by aggregate book value of equity on the universe of stocks that announce in a given month. Notice that aggregate earnings themselves are non-stationary and need to be scaled by something. I followVuolteenaho (2002)and use book value of equity, which could be thought of as a cumulative earnings measure over a long look-back window. This is because of the “clean accounting assumption,” which holds reasonably well in reality (Campbell 2017). This measure echoes the literature studying aggregate earnings (e.g.,Ball, Sadka, and Sadka 2009;Patatoukas 2014;Ball and Sadka 2015;Hann, Li, and Ogneva 2020), which shows that aggregate earnings convey important information for the economy. Overall, ROE is a simple manifestation of earnings, with which I can quantitatively illustrate the intuition in the previous section.

C. Evidence from Global Markets

C.1. Global Earnings Cycle

Having described the results in the United States, I move on to the global data. The U.S. stock market has been the world’s largest for decades, with high-quality market and accounting data readily available. It is therefore the most natural testing ground for financial theories in general. It is particularly relevant for my theory for two additional reasons: i) the earnings announcements in the United States occur much faster than in other countries, and ii) the U.S. market exerts an asymmetric influence on other markets (e.g.,Brusa, Savor, and Wilson 2020). These factors make the newsy months in the United States especially important, as they take information content away from the newsy months in other countries.

The global data are nonetheless valuable. Historically, major countries’ stock market capitalization is comparable to that of a U.S. sector. In addition to new data on stock returns, other countries have different earnings cycles from the United States and therefore can potentially provide meaningful out-of-sample tests of the theory. At least two sources of variation in the earnings cycles appear of interest. First is the variation in announcing frequency—specifically, a number of major countries require firms to announce semi-annually instead of quarterly. Second is the variation in the announcing lag—specifically, all major countries announce substantially more slowly than in the United States.

To look into the variation, I collect interim earnings announcement date, earnings, and book value of equity from Worldscope. Even though this data source has substantially better coverage of interim accounting data than Compustat Global, the data start only in the 1990s, and the coverage does not stabilize until 1998. This history is substantially shorter relative to the quarterly accounting data in the United States, where stable coverage begins in 1971. Perhaps this is part of the reason why studies on the U.S. market are more abundant. I nonetheless draw lessons from this international data set, as some patterns can be sufficiently clear even within a short sample of 20 years.

In terms of the variation in announcing frequency, a country-level tabulation exercise on fiscal periods’ end months reveals that, in those countries requiring semi-annual reporting, comparable numbers of firms have Mar/Sep and Jun/Dec as the end of fiscal periods. Hence, the entire country still operates on a quarterly calendar, and the first months of each quarter are still the earliest time for the freshest news to come out. The theory therefore applies to those countries as well.27

Regarding the variation in announcing lags, first, the left panel ofTable C1shows that the fiscal periods are also well aligned with the calendar quarters in the global sample. Hence, news in the first month of a quarter remains the freshest in the global data. However, the announcing intensity is very different and can be used to generate heterogeneity in the right-hand-side variables in the global analysis.Table C1shows that, relative to the United States, a much smaller fraction of the firms announce in the first months of the quarter in the global sample. In contrast, the second months contain the largest fraction of announcing firms—about 60%. Globally, it seems that both the first and the second months are viable candidates for newsy months, as the former provide the freshest news and the latter come with the most announcements. The third months, however, do not fall in either category. In fact, the average announcing lag in the United States is 37 days, which is a little longer than 1 month; that for the international sample is 55 days, which is about 2 months.28In other words, globally, the first months combined with the second months roughly constitute the first half of the earnings cycle. To enforce consistency across the United States and the global analysis, I set the newsy “months” to be the combined periods of the first and second months of a quarter in the global sample. For example, April and May combined is considered to be a newsy “month” in the global sample.29
