It is important to note that, while the practice of setting “newsy” months based on aggregate data is similar to what I did in the United States, doing so on the global sample involves a stronger assumption. In both the United States and the global sample, the return data start substantially earlier than the accounting data from which the newsy months are inferred. However, the history of accounting data is much longer in the United States, and it can be seen that over the course of this longer history, the aggregate earnings cycle remained stable and in fact was a few days faster in the earlier decades. Also, it is clearly documented that quarterly reporting has been present in the United States even years before 1926 thanks to the requirements of the NYSE (Kraft, Vashishtha, and Venkatachalam 2017). Outside the United States, similar evidence is less easy to find systematically in one place,30even though country-by-country research clearly shows that regular financial reporting had been ongoing decades before the inception of the Worldscope data, at least for financially developed nations such as the United Kingdom, Japan, France, and Australia.

Given these contexts, while I still use return data before the 1990s in my global tests, I focus on the financially developed countries, where financial regulation is sounder and has a longer history. The list of developed markets that I use follows that inAsness, Frazzini, and Pedersen (2019)and consists of the developed markets classified by Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI). Also, on this long sample of returns, I only use the simplest and clearest message from the global earnings announcement data, specifically the binary classification of which months are newsy.31Even with these practices, one should place more focus on the post-WWII and post-1974 results for the global sample and interpret the pre-1974 results with more caution.

C.2. Return Predictability

Having described the earnings-related information globally, I move on to the return predictability results. My country-level market returns come from Global Financial Data (GFD). Returns are all in U.S. dollars. A number of return series go back further than 1926, but in this analysis I truncate the sample at 1926 to be consistent with the U.S. results.

To start, panel A ofTable C2runs regressions analogous to those inTable 3, except they are now on a panel of country-level aggregate returns, and the newsy “months” now include both the first and second months of each calendar quarter. Column 1 shows an unconditional momentum effect. This reflects country-level momentum inMoskowitz, Ooi, and Pedersen (2012), which is in fact stronger in more recent periods. Columns 2 and 3 then show that this component is much stronger in the non-newsy months, here meaning the third month of each quarter. The result is robust in the post-WWII and post-1974 sample but very weak in the first half of the sample, perhaps reflecting the smaller sample size before 1974.

Since aggregate returns in other countries are positively related to the U.S. market returns contemporaneously, to observe the additional effect more cleanly, I remove each country’s loading on the U.S. market returns according to its U.S. return betas. I estimate the betas on a rolling 24-month basis up to 1 month before the dependent variable month, so that the beta does not contain any look-ahead bias, and the dependent variable corresponds to positions implementable in real time. The results are in panel B ofTable C2, and they convey a message similar to panel A. An analogous pattern can be found on the global panel of country-industry returns and is documented inSection IA.Eof theInternet Appendix.

Also,Table C3shows that patterns similar to those inTable 5exist in other countries according to their own earnings cycle. For each country, I determine the preseason period in the first months of the quarters by calculating the 0.5 percentile value of the announcement lag for that country. This value is then rounded down to the closest trading week to arrive at the length of the preseason. For the United States, because earnings announcement lags are comparatively short, this value converts to one trading week, the length used in the U.S. analysis above. For the other countries, earnings announcing is slower, and the preseason periods are often longer, ranging from 1 to 3 weeks. Column 2 shows that the market returns of these countries also do not reverse in their corresponding preseason periods. Column 3 shows that the reversal is quite strong immediately outside these preseason periods.
